nodes.label: parabolic dynamics
nodes.description: The study of dynamical systems with polynomial (sub-exponential) divergence of orbits. Sits between elliptic and hyperbolic dynamics; complexity shifts into renormalisation rather than phase-space expansion.
nodes.metaphor: Like a river that meanders rather than cascades — motion exists but without the dramatic divergence of turbulence.

nodes.label: random matrix products
nodes.description: Products of matrices drawn from a probability distribution. Central objects in the study of Lyapunov exponents and multiplicative ergodic theory.
nodes.metaphor: Like repeatedly multiplying distorted maps of space — small distortions compound into something statistically predictable.

nodes.label: multifractal analysis
nodes.description: The study of the dimensional structure of level sets of ergodic averages. Quantifies the heterogeneity of fractal measures; an open question asks whether analogous formalism applies to Rauzy-type systems.
nodes.metaphor: Like measuring a coastline at every possible scale simultaneously, and asking how the roughness changes.

nodes.label: stopping times
nodes.description: Random or deterministic times defined by first occurrence of an event (e.g. hitting a set), central to local statistical behaviour. Contrasts with mixing times, which describe global distributional convergence.
nodes.metaphor: A stopwatch that only stops when a specific milestone is reached, measuring local trajectory events rather than global averages.

nodes.label: Lyapunov exponents
nodes.description: Quantities measuring the average exponential rate of divergence of nearby trajectories. The bridge between random matrix products and dynamical behaviour; governed by the Kontsevich–Zorich cocycle in Teichmüller dynamics.
nodes.metaphor: Like measuring how fast two nearly-identical weather forecasts diverge — the rate at which small errors become big ones.

nodes.label: spectrum as compressed geometry
nodes.description: Spectral data acts as a compressed encoding of geometric and dynamical structure, preserving some invariants but losing full detail. Highlighted by Kac's question and wave propagation in billiard domains.
nodes.metaphor: Like a musical chord that encodes the shape of an instrument — you can hear something about the geometry without seeing it.

nodes.label: dynamics vs renormalisation duality
nodes.description: When dynamics lacks expansion (parabolic systems), complexity shifts from phase space into renormalisation or parameter space. Seen in comparison between polygonal billiards, Rauzy induction, and hyperbolic systems.
nodes.metaphor: Like a sculpture that looks simple from far away but reveals intricate structure only in the tooling process, not the final form.

nodes.label: infinite renormalisation as irreducibility
nodes.description: Infinite renormalisability signals that a system cannot be reduced to a finite description and retains structure across all scales. Evidenced by Rauzy induction, continued fractions, and the Rauzy gasket.
nodes.metaphor: Like a fractal that always reveals new structure when you zoom in — it refuses to resolve into something simpler.

nodes.label: wave–particle dual encoding
nodes.description: Linear wave phenomena encode underlying nonlinear trajectory dynamics, with periodic orbits leaving measurable spectral signatures. Formalised by trace formulas in billiard and quantum contexts.
nodes.metaphor: Like hearing the drumbeat of footsteps through a wall — the wave carries information about the discrete thing that created it.

nodes.label: parameter-space fractals replace phase-space fractals
nodes.description: In non-expanding systems, fractal structure emerges in parameter space via renormalisation rather than in phase space. Contrasts Rauzy gasket (parameter) with Cantor repellers (phase space).
nodes.metaphor: Like finding that the map of possible routes is more intricate than any single journey — the complexity lives in the choice space, not the path.

nodes.label: spectral geometry
nodes.description: Study of how geometric properties of a domain are encoded in the eigenvalues of its Laplacian. Foundational question: can one hear the shape of a drum?
nodes.metaphor: Like asking whether you can reconstruct a drum from its sound alone — using frequencies to infer shape.

nodes.label: Laplacian spectrum
nodes.description: The set of eigenvalues of the Laplace–Beltrami operator on a domain, governing wave and heat behaviour. The central object of spectral geometry.
nodes.metaphor: Like the natural frequencies of a shape — the tones it would ring at if struck.

nodes.label: billiard flow
nodes.description: Hamiltonian dynamical system of a particle reflecting off the boundary of a domain. Periodic billiard trajectories contribute to spectral trace formulas.
nodes.metaphor: Like a snooker ball on a frictionless table with perfectly elastic walls, studied over infinite time.

nodes.label: trace formula
nodes.description: A relation expressing spectral data (eigenvalue sums) in terms of periodic orbits of the underlying classical system. Bridges quantum and classical descriptions.
nodes.metaphor: Like a dictionary between two languages — each entry on the spectral side corresponds to an entry on the orbit side.

nodes.label: polygonal billiards
nodes.description: Billiard flow in a polygon, leading to parabolic dynamics with singularities at corners. Connects to translation surfaces via the unfolding construction.
nodes.metaphor: Like billiards on a table with straight walls but awkward corners — the corners create the difficulty.

nodes.label: translation surface
nodes.description: A flat surface with cone-angle singularities obtained by unfolding a rational billiard. The natural geometric object for studying IETs and Teichmüller dynamics.
nodes.metaphor: Like unfolding a mirror-maze into a flat surface by keeping track of where each reflection glues to.

nodes.label: interval exchange transformation (IET)
nodes.description: A piecewise isometry of an interval encoding first return maps of directional flows on translation surfaces. Generalises circle rotations.
nodes.metaphor: Like cutting a ribbon into pieces and rearranging them in a fixed order — a piecewise rigid shuffle.

nodes.label: Rauzy induction
nodes.description: A renormalisation procedure for interval exchange transformations, analogous to the Euclidean algorithm. Acts as a discrete Poincaré section of the Teichmüller flow.
nodes.metaphor: Like the Euclidean algorithm for rhythms — repeatedly subtracting the smaller from the larger to reveal the irreducible pattern.

nodes.label: Teichmüller flow
nodes.description: Continuous deformation of translation surfaces via directional stretching. Drives the dynamics on moduli spaces of translation surfaces.
nodes.metaphor: Like stretching a rubber sheet in one direction while compressing in another, and watching how the geometry deforms.

nodes.label: Rauzy gasket
nodes.description: A fractal subset of the IET parameter space defined by infinite renormalisability under Rauzy induction. Analogue of a Sierpiński-type fractal in this setting.
nodes.metaphor: Like the Cantor set but for three-interval shuffles — the set of all patterns that never simplify under renormalisation.

nodes.label: continued fractions / Gauss map
nodes.description: Renormalisation scheme for circle rotations, providing the 1D model for infinite renormalisation. The Gauss map is the corresponding dynamical system on the parameter space.
nodes.metaphor: Like peeling an onion of approximations — each step reveals how well a number is approximated by rationals at that scale.

nodes.label: Kontsevich–Zorich cocycle
nodes.description: A cocycle over the Teichmüller flow describing growth rates of relative cohomology. Its Lyapunov exponents govern deviation phenomena in translation surface dynamics.
nodes.metaphor: Like tracking how a vector rotates and stretches as you follow the Teichmüller flow — a bookkeeping device for deviation.

nodes.label: substitution tilings
nodes.description: Aperiodic tilings generated by inflation rules, producing fractal structures via repeated scaling. Canonical example: Penrose tilings.
nodes.metaphor: Like a sentence generated by a grammar that replaces each word with a fixed phrase — the pattern repeats at every scale.

nodes.label: multifractality in Rauzy-type systems?
nodes.description: Is there a multifractal formalism or spectrum associated to Rauzy induction or the Rauzy gasket, analogous to hyperbolic systems? Would unify parabolic renormalisation with thermodynamic formalism.

nodes.label: spectral interpretation of polygonal billiards?
nodes.description: How precisely do orbit family degeneracies in polygonal billiards affect spectral statistics and trace formulas? Clarifies limits of wave–particle correspondence in non-hyperbolic settings.

nodes.label: translation surfaces and aperiodic order?
nodes.description: Under what conditions do translation surfaces derived from tilings exhibit genuine aperiodic order in their dynamics? Connects billiards, tilings, and quasicrystal-type structures.

nodes.label: bounded-type analogues in Rauzy dynamics?
nodes.description: What is the precise analogue of bounded continued fractions in Rauzy induction, and how does it control dynamical deviation? Would link quantitative ergodic properties to the irrational analogy.

nodes.label: Kac (1966)
nodes.description: Foundational paper framing the inverse spectral problem: do Laplacian eigenvalues determine a domain up to isometry? ('Can one hear the shape of a drum?')

nodes.label: Gordon–Webb–Wolpert (1992)
nodes.description: Constructed isospectral but non-isometric domains, answering Kac's question negatively.

nodes.label: Hermann Weyl
nodes.description: Established the asymptotic relationship between the eigenvalue counting function and domain volume (Weyl's law).

nodes.label: Masur–Veech theory
nodes.description: Describes ergodicity and invariant measures for IETs and translation surfaces; foundational for the modern theory.

nodes.label: Gutzwiller trace formula
nodes.description: Connects quantum spectral data with classical periodic orbits; the foundational tool for wave–particle dual encoding.

nodes.label: critical orbit controls everything
nodes.description: The global dynamics of a quadratic polynomial is governed entirely by the behaviour of its single critical orbit.
nodes.metaphor: Like a single hinge that determines how an entire folding structure moves.

nodes.label: escape vs feedback dichotomy
nodes.description: Dynamics splits into two regimes: escape leads to no feedback and simple expansion, while boundedness creates feedback and complex structure.
nodes.metaphor: Like heat dissipating into space versus being trapped and recycled in a closed system.

nodes.label: expansion needs no recurrence
nodes.description: Uniform expansion occurs precisely when the critical orbit does not return to influence the system.
nodes.metaphor: Like a flow that keeps stretching without ever folding back on itself.

nodes.label: recurrence creates structure
nodes.description: When the critical orbit is bounded and recurrent, it continuously injects complexity, producing connected and structured dynamics.
nodes.metaphor: Like repeatedly folding dough to build layers.

nodes.label: parabolic incompatible with disconnection
nodes.description: Parabolic behaviour requires critical recurrence, which is incompatible with totally disconnected Julia sets.
nodes.metaphor: A fixed point that traps motion cannot coexist with a system where everything flies apart.

nodes.label: quadratic polynomial dynamics
nodes.description: Iteration of maps f_c(z) = z² + c, where all dynamical complexity is controlled by a single critical point.
nodes.metaphor: A machine with one key lever — the critical orbit — controlling all behaviour.

nodes.label: critical orbit
nodes.description: The forward orbit of the critical point of a map, which determines global dynamical behaviour in one-dimensional complex dynamics.
nodes.metaphor: The source of all folding and complexity — the one trajectory that decides everything.

nodes.label: Julia set
nodes.description: The boundary between stable and chaotic dynamics, whose geometry directly reflects the behaviour of the critical orbit.
nodes.metaphor: The visible shadow of the system's instability — connected when the critical orbit is trapped, dust when it escapes.

nodes.label: Fatou set
nodes.description: The complementary set to the Julia set, consisting of points with stable, predictable long-term behaviour.
nodes.metaphor: The calm regions surrounding the chaotic boundary.

nodes.label: Mandelbrot set
nodes.description: The parameter set of c-values for which the critical orbit of f_c remains bounded, equivalently where the Julia set is connected.
nodes.metaphor: A complete atlas of all possible bounded behaviours, organised by what the critical orbit does.

nodes.label: uniform expansion
nodes.description: A dynamical regime where all derivatives grow exponentially, yielding hyperbolic behaviour on the Julia set.
nodes.metaphor: A rubber sheet being stretched evenly everywhere with no folding.

nodes.label: parabolic cycle
nodes.description: A periodic point with multiplier exactly 1, where expansion breaks down and the system exhibits delicate parabolic behaviour.
nodes.metaphor: A point where motion neither escapes nor contracts but lingers indefinitely.

nodes.label: symbolic dynamics interpretation?
nodes.description: How does the escape vs bounded dichotomy translate into symbolic dynamics — kneading theory or pruning?

nodes.label: Douady–Hubbard theory
nodes.description: Foundational theory establishing the link between critical orbits, the Mandelbrot set, and Julia set connectivity.

nodes.label: stopping vs averaging dichotomy
nodes.description: Stopping times describe event-based, trajectory-level behaviour; mixing and limit theorems describe averaged asymptotic behaviour over the whole system.
nodes.metaphor: Asking when you reach a destination versus asking what traffic looks like on average across the city.

nodes.label: harmonic functions encode stopping data
nodes.description: Harmonic functions naturally encode hitting probabilities and expected stopping times through mean-value and Poisson equations.
nodes.metaphor: A temperature field where the value at each point already contains the likelihood of where heat will eventually flow.

nodes.label: electrical flows mirror random walks
nodes.description: Electrical quantities — current, voltage, resistance — directly correspond to expected traversal behaviour and stopping statistics of random walks.
nodes.metaphor: A random walker behaves like electrical current spreading through a circuit, favouring low-resistance paths.

nodes.label: global vs local statistics split
nodes.description: Global statistical properties arise from distributional convergence across the space; local properties arise from first-passage events and pointwise fluctuations.
nodes.metaphor: Climate versus weather — long-run averages versus what happens at a specific place and time.

nodes.label: variational principles unify structures
nodes.description: Both thermodynamic and electrical frameworks are governed by optimisation principles, suggesting a shared structural language for dynamics and probability.
nodes.metaphor: Different physical systems all settling into the most economical configuration under their own rules.

nodes.label: mixing time
nodes.description: The timescale over which a system's distribution approaches its invariant measure, capturing global convergence speed.
nodes.metaphor: How long it takes for dye dropped in water to become uniformly spread.

nodes.label: cover time
nodes.description: The expected time for a random walk to visit all vertices of a graph.
nodes.metaphor: Time needed to explore every room in a building at least once.

nodes.label: blanket time
nodes.description: The time until empirical visitation frequencies approximate the stationary distribution.
nodes.metaphor: When your travel history starts to look like a fair sampling of the whole city.

nodes.label: harmonic functions (graph)
nodes.description: Functions satisfying a mean-value property on graphs, encoding hitting probabilities and solving boundary value problems.
nodes.metaphor: A perfectly balanced surface where each point's value is determined entirely by its neighbours.

nodes.label: electrical network theory
nodes.description: Framework translating graph-based random walks into flows, voltages, and resistances, enabling exact computation of stopping statistics.
nodes.metaphor: Viewing a graph as a circuit where random movement follows energy-efficient pathways.

nodes.label: effective resistance
nodes.description: A metric on graphs governing commute times and accessibility; the resistance between two nodes equals their expected commute time.
nodes.metaphor: How hard it is for current — or a random walker — to travel between two points.

nodes.label: graph Laplacian
nodes.description: The discrete operator governing harmonicity and diffusion on graphs, analogous to but distinct from the Laplace–Beltrami operator on manifolds.
nodes.metaphor: A discrete curvature measuring how each point differs from the average of its neighbours.

nodes.label: transfer operator
nodes.description: Operator describing the evolution of densities under a dynamical system; the engine of thermodynamic formalism.
nodes.metaphor: A machine that redistributes mass according to how the system stretches and folds space.

nodes.label: thermodynamic formalism
nodes.description: Framework relating entropy, pressure, and invariant measures via variational principles; foundational for ergodic theory and multifractal analysis.
nodes.metaphor: Assigning energy and temperature to orbits to understand their long-run statistical behaviour.

nodes.label: electrical formalism for dynamics?
nodes.description: Can electrical network concepts — resistance, flow, harmonicity — be systematically translated into a framework for dynamical systems and their stopping-time analogues?

nodes.label: harmonic approach to return times?
nodes.description: Can return times in deterministic dynamical systems be characterised via harmonic or Poisson-type equations analogous to those in random walks?

nodes.label: dynamical resistance metric?
nodes.description: Is there a notion of effective resistance in dynamical systems capturing orbit connectivity or transport difficulty?

nodes.label: Doyle & Snell — Random Walks and Electric Networks
nodes.description: Establishes the core correspondence between electrical networks and random walks; foundational text.

nodes.label: Aldous & Fill (monograph)
nodes.description: Comprehensive reference for hitting times, mixing, and electrical methods in Markov chains.

edges.explanation: Lyapunov exponents are the primary quantities computed from random matrix products via the Furstenberg–Kesten theorem.
edges.sentence: Random matrix products give rise to Lyapunov exponents via the Furstenberg–Kesten theorem, which governs their almost-sure growth rate.

edges.explanation: Vanishing or zero Lyapunov exponents characterise the parabolic regime; understanding them is central to parabolic dynamics.
edges.sentence: Lyapunov exponents are a key tool for parabolic dynamics, since their vanishing or near-vanishing characterises the parabolic regime.

edges.explanation: Multifractal spectra of Lyapunov exponents describe the fine structure of hyperbolic and partially hyperbolic systems.
edges.sentence: Multifractal analysis is a tool for studying Lyapunov exponents, describing the dimensional structure of their level sets.

edges.explanation: Return time statistics and inducing schemes use stopping times to convert parabolic systems into more tractable hyperbolic ones.
edges.sentence: Stopping times are used in parabolic dynamics via inducing schemes, converting slow systems into faster hyperbolic ones.

edges.explanation: Both encode complex structure via compressed representations — spectral frequencies versus renormalisation sequences.
edges.sentence: Spectrum as compressed geometry is analogous to Rauzy induction — both encode complex structure into a compressed representation, one via frequencies and one via renormalisation sequences.

edges.explanation: Periodic billiard trajectories determine spectral features of a domain via trace formulas.
edges.sentence: Billiard flow is a tool for spectral geometry, since periodic orbits of the flow determine the domain's spectral features via trace formulas.

edges.explanation: Directional flows on polygons correspond to IETs via first return maps to transversals.
edges.sentence: Polygonal billiards are analogous to interval exchange transformations, connected via first return maps of directional flows.

edges.explanation: Rauzy induction acts as a discrete Poincaré section of the continuous Teichmüller flow on moduli space.
edges.sentence: Rauzy induction is a tool for studying the Teichmüller flow, acting as its discrete Poincaré section on moduli space.

edges.explanation: The Rauzy gasket is the canonical example of a parameter-space fractal arising from infinite renormalisation.
edges.sentence: The Rauzy gasket is a concrete instance of the intuition that parameter-space fractals replace phase-space fractals in parabolic systems.

edges.explanation: Continued fractions / the Gauss map provide the 1D model for infinite renormalisation; Rauzy induction is the higher-dimensional generalisation.
edges.sentence: Continued fractions are a special case of Rauzy induction, providing the one-dimensional model whose higher-dimensional generalisation Rauzy induction constitutes.

edges.explanation: Both produce fractal structures via repeated scaling rules — substitution tilings in physical space, the Rauzy gasket in parameter space.
edges.sentence: Substitution tilings unexpectedly relate to the Rauzy gasket — both produce fractal structure through repeated scaling, one in physical space and one in parameter space.

edges.explanation: Infinite structure resists finite spectral encoding, highlighting fundamental limits of spectral compression as a description of complex dynamics.
edges.sentence: Infinite renormalisation as irreducibility is in tension with spectrum as compressed geometry — infinite multi-scale structure resists finite spectral encoding.

edges.explanation: Trace formulas are the mathematical formalisation of wave–particle dual encoding: they express spectral sums in terms of periodic orbits.
edges.sentence: Wave–particle dual encoding is the intuition formalised by trace formulas, which express spectral sums in terms of classical periodic orbits.

edges.explanation: The Gutzwiller trace formula is the canonical result instantiating wave–particle correspondence for quantum systems.
edges.sentence: The Gutzwiller trace formula is a context for the trace formula concept, providing the canonical quantum-classical instance of wave–particle correspondence.

edges.explanation: Kac's 1966 paper is the foundational framing of the inverse spectral problem that defines spectral geometry as a field.
edges.sentence: Kac's 1966 paper is the foundational context for spectral geometry, posing the inverse spectral problem that defines the field.

edges.explanation: Gordon–Webb–Wolpert resolved Kac's question negatively, defining a key boundary of spectral geometry.
edges.sentence: Gordon–Webb–Wolpert (1992) is a key result in spectral geometry, resolving Kac's question negatively by constructing isospectral non-isometric domains.

edges.explanation: Masur–Veech theory provides the ergodic theory of IETs and translation surfaces, including existence of invariant measures.
edges.sentence: Masur–Veech theory is the foundational context for interval exchange transformations, establishing ergodicity and the existence of invariant measures.

edges.explanation: Polygonal billiards are a primary example of parabolic dynamics — polynomial divergence arising from corner singularities.
edges.sentence: Polygonal billiards are a primary example of parabolic dynamics, exhibiting polynomial orbit divergence from corner singularities.

edges.explanation: Translation surfaces arise from unfolding rational billiards, converting reflection dynamics into linear flows.
edges.sentence: Translation surfaces are a tool for studying polygonal billiards, arising from the unfolding construction that converts reflection dynamics into linear flows.

edges.explanation: IETs encode the first return maps of directional flows on translation surfaces; they are the combinatorial face of the geometric object.
edges.sentence: Interval exchange transformations are a tool for translation surfaces, encoding their directional flows as combinatorial first return maps.

edges.explanation: The intuition directly characterises what makes parabolic dynamics hard: the action is in renormalisation space, not phase space. (inferred)
edges.sentence: The intuition of dynamics vs renormalisation duality is a context for parabolic dynamics — it explains why the complexity of parabolic systems lives in renormalisation space rather than phase space.

edges.explanation: An open question asks whether Rauzy induction admits a multifractal formalism analogous to hyperbolic systems — a potential deep connection. (inferred)
edges.sentence: Rauzy induction unexpectedly relates to multifractal analysis — an open question asks whether a multifractal formalism analogous to hyperbolic systems exists for Rauzy-type dynamics. (inferred)

edges.explanation: The Lyapunov exponents of the Kontsevich–Zorich cocycle govern deviation phenomena in translation surface dynamics. (inferred)
edges.sentence: Lyapunov exponents are a context for the Kontsevich–Zorich cocycle — the cocycle's Lyapunov spectrum governs deviation phenomena in translation surface dynamics. (inferred)

edges.explanation: The question asks whether multifractal analysis extends to Rauzy-type systems.
edges.sentence: The question about multifractality in Rauzy-type systems directly concerns multifractal analysis and whether its formalism extends beyond the hyperbolic setting.

edges.explanation: The question targets Rauzy induction as the system for which a multifractal formalism is sought.
edges.sentence: The question about multifractality in Rauzy-type systems targets Rauzy induction as the system for which a multifractal thermodynamic formalism is sought.

edges.explanation: The question concerns spectral statistics of polygonal billiards specifically.
edges.sentence: The question about spectral interpretation of polygonal billiards concerns how orbit degeneracies specific to polygonal billiards affect trace formula predictions.

edges.explanation: The question asks how orbit degeneracies in polygonal billiards affect trace formula predictions.
edges.sentence: The question about spectral interpretation of polygonal billiards asks how orbit degeneracies affect the trace formula in non-hyperbolic settings.

edges.explanation: The question concerns which translation surfaces exhibit aperiodic order.
edges.sentence: The question about translation surfaces and aperiodic order asks which translation surfaces exhibit genuine quasicrystal-type dynamics.

edges.explanation: The question connects translation surfaces derived from tiling constructions to aperiodic order.
edges.sentence: The question about translation surfaces and aperiodic order connects translation surfaces derived from substitution tilings to aperiodic order.

edges.explanation: The question seeks the Rauzy analogue of bounded continued fractions.
edges.sentence: The question about bounded-type analogues in Rauzy dynamics asks for the Rauzy analogue of bounded continued fractions.

edges.explanation: Bounded continued fractions are the 1D model whose Rauzy analogue the question seeks.
edges.sentence: The question about bounded-type analogues in Rauzy dynamics seeks to generalise the theory of bounded continued fractions to the Rauzy setting.

edges.explanation: The Laplacian spectrum is the central object of spectral geometry.
edges.sentence: The Laplacian spectrum is the central object of spectral geometry — it is the collection of eigenvalues whose structure encodes geometric properties of the domain.

edges.explanation: The intuition is directly instantiated by the Laplacian spectrum as a compressed encoding of domain geometry.
edges.sentence: The intuition that spectrum acts as compressed geometry is directly instantiated by the Laplacian spectrum, which preserves some geometric invariants while losing others.
The intuition that the critical orbit controls everything is instantiated most sharply in quadratic polynomial dynamics, where there is only one critical point.
edges.explanation: The intuition that the critical orbit controls everything is instantiated most sharply in quadratic polynomial dynamics, where there is only one critical point.

edges.sentence: The escape vs feedback dichotomy is analogous to uniform expansion: escape removes recurrence, which is precisely the condition for hyperbolicity.
edges.explanation: The escape vs feedback dichotomy is analogous to uniform expansion: escape removes recurrence, which is precisely the condition for hyperbolicity.

edges.sentence: Recurrence creates structure is in tension with uniform expansion — recurrence forces the breakdown of uniform hyperbolicity.
edges.explanation: Recurrence creates structure is in tension with uniform expansion — recurrence forces the breakdown of uniform hyperbolicity.

edges.sentence: Parabolic incompatible with disconnection is grounded in Mandelbrot set theory: parabolic behaviour implies bounded critical orbit, hence membership in the Mandelbrot set.
edges.explanation: Parabolic incompatible with disconnection is grounded in Mandelbrot set theory: parabolic behaviour implies bounded critical orbit, hence membership in the Mandelbrot set.

edges.sentence: The critical orbit determines the Julia set: it is connected precisely when the critical orbit is bounded.
edges.explanation: The critical orbit determines the Julia set: it is connected precisely when the critical orbit is bounded.

edges.sentence: The Mandelbrot set is defined by the fate of the critical orbit — membership means the orbit remains bounded.
edges.explanation: The Mandelbrot set is defined by the fate of the critical orbit — membership means the orbit remains bounded.

edges.sentence: The Julia set and Fatou set are complementary partitions of the Riemann sphere — the Julia set is the boundary of the Fatou set.
edges.explanation: The Julia set and Fatou set are complementary partitions of the Riemann sphere — the Julia set is the boundary of the Fatou set.

edges.sentence: Parabolic cycles are the prototypical local feature of parabolic dynamics in complex analysis — they are the points where the parabolic regime is realised.
edges.explanation: Parabolic cycles are the prototypical local feature of parabolic dynamics in complex analysis — they are the points where the parabolic regime is realised.

edges.sentence: Douady–Hubbard theory is the foundational context for the Mandelbrot set, establishing the equivalence between critical orbit boundedness and Julia set connectivity.
edges.explanation: Douady–Hubbard theory is the foundational context for the Mandelbrot set, establishing the equivalence between critical orbit boundedness and Julia set connectivity.

edges.sentence: The question about symbolic dynamics interpretation asks how the fate of the critical orbit translates into combinatorial models.
edges.explanation: The question about symbolic dynamics interpretation asks how the fate of the critical orbit translates into combinatorial models.

edges.sentence: The Mandelbrot set is the canonical example of the parameter-space fractals intuition in complex dynamics — its fractal boundary encodes all possible critical orbit behaviours. (inferred)
edges.explanation: The Mandelbrot set is the canonical example of the parameter-space fractals intuition in complex dynamics — its fractal boundary encodes all possible critical orbit behaviours. (inferred)

edges.sentence: Julia set connectivity is the phase-space face of the dynamics vs renormalisation duality — connected vs disconnected reflects whether complexity is concentrated in phase space or renormalisation. (inferred)
edges.explanation: Julia set connectivity is the phase-space face of the dynamics vs renormalisation duality — connected vs disconnected reflects whether complexity is concentrated in phase space or renormalisation. (inferred)

edges.sentence: Parabolic cycles are characterised by zero Lyapunov exponents — they are precisely the dynamical configurations where exponential divergence breaks down. (inferred)
edges.explanation: Parabolic cycles are characterised by zero Lyapunov exponents — they are precisely the dynamical configurations where exponential divergence breaks down. (inferred)

edges.sentence: Harmonic functions on graphs are a tool for computing stopping times — they solve the boundary value problems encoding hitting probabilities and expected stopping times.
edges.explanation: Harmonic functions on graphs are a tool for computing stopping times — they solve the boundary value problems encoding hitting probabilities and expected stopping times.

edges.sentence: Electrical network theory is a tool for bounding cover times — resistance and flow estimates provide sharp bounds on graph exploration times.
edges.explanation: Electrical network theory is a tool for bounding cover times — resistance and flow estimates provide sharp bounds on graph exploration times.

edges.sentence: Effective resistance is a tool for understanding mixing time — low resistance between nodes correlates with rapid spreading and fast mixing.
edges.explanation: Effective resistance is a tool for understanding mixing time — low resistance between nodes correlates with rapid spreading and fast mixing.

edges.sentence: Stopping times are in tension with mixing time — one focuses on the first occurrence of specific events while the other captures distributional convergence across the whole system.
edges.explanation: Stopping times are in tension with mixing time — one focuses on the first occurrence of specific events while the other captures distributional convergence across the whole system.

edges.sentence: The transfer operator is analogous to the graph Laplacian — both describe how distributions evolve toward equilibrium, one for densities under a dynamical system and one for functions on a graph.
edges.explanation: The transfer operator is analogous to the graph Laplacian — both describe how distributions evolve toward equilibrium, one for densities under a dynamical system and one for functions on a graph.

edges.sentence: Thermodynamic formalism is analogous to electrical network theory — both are governed by variational principles, pressure minimisation in one case and energy minimisation in the other.
edges.explanation: Thermodynamic formalism is analogous to electrical network theory — both are governed by variational principles, pressure minimisation in one case and energy minimisation in the other.

edges.sentence: Blanket time unexpectedly relates to mixing time — it links trajectory-level sampling behaviour to global distributional convergence in a precise quantitative sense.
edges.explanation: Blanket time unexpectedly relates to mixing time — it links trajectory-level sampling behaviour to global distributional convergence in a precise quantitative sense.

edges.sentence: The stopping vs averaging dichotomy is the conceptual context for stopping times, capturing the contrast between local event-based statistics and global averages.
edges.explanation: The stopping vs averaging dichotomy is the conceptual context for stopping times, capturing the contrast between local event-based statistics and global averages.

edges.sentence: The stopping vs averaging dichotomy is the conceptual context for mixing time, placing it on the global averaging side of the split.
edges.explanation: The stopping vs averaging dichotomy is the conceptual context for mixing time, placing it on the global averaging side of the split.

edges.sentence: The global vs local statistics split is instantiated by stopping times as the local side — first-passage events are pointwise, trajectory-level phenomena.
edges.explanation: The global vs local statistics split is instantiated by stopping times as the local side — first-passage events are pointwise, trajectory-level phenomena.

edges.sentence: The global vs local statistics split is instantiated by mixing time as the global side — it measures distributional convergence across the whole space.
edges.explanation: The global vs local statistics split is instantiated by mixing time as the global side — it measures distributional convergence across the whole space.

edges.sentence: The intuition that harmonic functions encode stopping data is concretely instantiated by harmonic functions on graphs, which solve Dirichlet problems for hitting probabilities.
edges.explanation: The intuition that harmonic functions encode stopping data is concretely instantiated by harmonic functions on graphs, which solve Dirichlet problems for hitting probabilities.

edges.sentence: The intuition that electrical flows mirror random walks is the conceptual core of electrical network theory as applied to Markov chains.
edges.explanation: The intuition that electrical flows mirror random walks is the conceptual core of electrical network theory as applied to Markov chains.

edges.sentence: The variational principles intuition is exemplified by thermodynamic formalism, which defines pressure and equilibrium measures via a variational principle.
edges.explanation: The variational principles intuition is exemplified by thermodynamic formalism, which defines pressure and equilibrium measures via a variational principle.

edges.sentence: The variational principles intuition is exemplified by electrical network theory, where Kirchhoff's laws emerge from energy minimisation.
edges.explanation: The variational principles intuition is exemplified by electrical network theory, where Kirchhoff's laws emerge from energy minimisation.

edges.sentence: The question about electrical formalism for dynamics asks whether electrical network theory can be extended to deterministic dynamical systems.
edges.explanation: The question about electrical formalism for dynamics asks whether electrical network theory can be extended to deterministic dynamical systems.

edges.sentence: The question about electrical formalism for dynamics specifically targets stopping-time analogues in dynamical systems.
edges.explanation: The question about electrical formalism for dynamics specifically targets stopping-time analogues in dynamical systems.

edges.sentence: The question about harmonic approach to return times asks whether harmonic function methods extend from random walks to deterministic recurrence.
edges.explanation: The question about harmonic approach to return times asks whether harmonic function methods extend from random walks to deterministic recurrence.

edges.sentence: The question about a dynamical resistance metric asks whether effective resistance has a meaningful analogue in dynamical systems.
edges.explanation: The question about a dynamical resistance metric asks whether effective resistance has a meaningful analogue in dynamical systems.

edges.sentence: Doyle & Snell is the foundational reference establishing electrical network theory as a tool for random walk analysis.
edges.explanation: Doyle & Snell is the foundational reference establishing electrical network theory as a tool for random walk analysis.

edges.sentence: Thermodynamic formalism is a primary tool for multifractal analysis — the multifractal spectrum is computed via pressure functions and equilibrium measures. (inferred)
edges.explanation: Thermodynamic formalism is a primary tool for multifractal analysis — the multifractal spectrum is computed via pressure functions and equilibrium measures. (inferred)

edges.sentence: The transfer operator is the engine of thermodynamic formalism — equilibrium measures and pressure are computed as spectral data of the transfer operator. (inferred)
edges.explanation: The transfer operator is the engine of thermodynamic formalism — equilibrium measures and pressure are computed as spectral data of the transfer operator. (inferred)

edges.sentence: The graph Laplacian is analogous to the Laplace–Beltrami operator whose eigenvalues form the Laplacian spectrum — one is discrete, the other continuous. (inferred)
edges.explanation: The graph Laplacian is analogous to the Laplace–Beltrami operator whose eigenvalues form the Laplacian spectrum — one is discrete, the other continuous. (inferred)

edges.sentence: Harmonic functions on graphs are analogous to harmonic analysis in spectral geometry — both use eigenfunctions of a Laplacian to encode geometric and probabilistic information. (inferred)
edges.explanation: Harmonic functions on graphs are analogous to harmonic analysis in spectral geometry — both use eigenfunctions of a Laplacian to encode geometric and probabilistic information. (inferred)
